text: For those patients undergoing ilioanal pouch failure in whom diversion with ileostomy alone is inadequate or inappropriate, pouch excision is required.
The contemporary series of ilioanal pouch excisions at St Mark's has been recently published in Diseases of the Colon and Rectum, including a detailed description of the steps involved.
In this video, Professor Sue Clark demonstrates the approach, which has become standardised over time in a high-volume centre.
Ureteric catheters are inserted at the beginning of the procedure, likely to aid in identifying and protecting the ureters during surgery.
We're careful to explain to the patient that these do not guarantee the safety of the ureters but do make them easier to identify and protect.
It's important to position the patient to allow good access to the perineum.
A rolled-up towel is placed under the sacrum and covered with a plastic sheet, providing support and protection during the procedure.
The patient's arms are wrapped and secured at their sides, ensuring they remain stationary during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully enters the abdomen, performing extensive adhesiolysis to provide sufficient access to the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the bowel just above the pouch and divides it using a linear stapler, visibly separating the bowel segment.
The surgeon holds the blind end of the pouch with a Babcock clamp, providing stability for further manipulation.
The surgeon washes out the pouch, ensuring it is clean if it has not been previously defunctioned.
A flexible plastic catheter is connected to an enema bag filled with fluid using a connector, preparing for irrigation or fluid introduction into the surgical site.
The surgeon instills fluid into the pouch through the flexible plastic catheter, allowing it to drain out without removing the catheter.
The surgeon uses a plastic clamp to control the flow of fluid through the catheter connected to the pouch.
A mixture of saline and povidone iodine is instilled into the pouch using a flexible plastic catheter until the pouch is visibly full, then drained out into a bucket.
The surgeon ensures the pouch is completely emptied, repeating the process until the effluent runs clear, indicating thorough cleansing.
The abdominal operator observes the pouch visibly filling with fluid, indicating the completion of the instillation process.
The surgeon applies pressure to the pouch to ensure it is fully emptied of fluid.
The surgeon clears adhesions from the mesentery of the prepouch ileum, identifying the plane between the mesentery and the retroperitoneum.
Great care is needed to identify and protect the ureters during this manoeuvre.
The surgeon opens a window between the small bowel mesentery and the retroperitoneum using a surgical clip, then passes a tape through the created space.
The surgeon inspects and palpates the area, confirming that both ureters are positioned behind the tape, while the superior mesenteric artery is located in front.

The surgeon continues dissecting the small bowel mesentery from the retroperitoneum. Traction on the tape elevates the mesentery and superior mesenteric vessels, enhancing access to the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the mesentery between clips, ensuring the division is perpendicular to the bowel to prevent proximal movement and avoid devascularizing the bowel above the pouch.
The surgeon transfixes the superior mesenteric artery pedicle using a proline suture, securing the vessel in place.
The surgeon packs the bowel proximal to the pouch away in the upper abdomen and places retractors to enhance access to the pelvic region.
The surgeon dissects the pouch free down to the pelvic floor, ensuring it is fully mobilized from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs dissection in the extrasphincteric plane, unless the pouch anal anastomosis was completed with mucosectomy and hand-sewn technique, which allows for intersphincteric dissection.
The video shows a stapled anastomosis in the intersphincteric plane, with visible fibrosis. Attempts to dissect this plane often result in breaching the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses the St Mark's self-retaining retractor to facilitate deeper dissection in the pelvis. An energy device is employed to assist with the dissection, particularly due to the indurated nature of the pelvic floor tissue.
The surgeon completes the posterior and lateral dissection, allowing the pouch to be delivered and facilitating the subsequent anterior dissection.
The surgeon carefully examines the pouch and anal canal for integrity, visually inspecting for any residual pouch wall that may need removal.
The surgeon closes the subcutaneous tissue in layers using an absorbable suture, followed by the closure of the perineal skin with a soft absorbable suture in an interrupted fashion.
The surgeon applies glue to the surgical site for protection, leaving the area without any dressing.
The surgeon removes the ureteric catheters and proceeds to close the abdomen, completing the surgical procedure.
A suction drain is placed in the pelvis, visible within the surgical field.
We hope this video was informative.
Please see the published manuscript for further information on a series of ninety two patients and for further detail on the operative technique.
